That part can be explained, Xenomorphs only kill on sight when they are threatened, they usually take their time to incapacitate isolated targets to inseminate them. We have to take into account that the Xenomorph in Alien Earth knows there are eggs in the ship and it's trying to :

1) Deal with the potential threat and kill as many intruders as it can
2) Create more Xenomorphs

That's why after butchering everyone it was about to capture Joe instead of killing him and why it didn't go full Berserk on him, the same thing happened with the Cyborg after killing everyone it was alone with its prey and took its time.

The writing is not perfect but that part made sense

replies.text:
Issues with this show:

1. Its “tell, DONT show”
Its backwards????
Alot of things happen off screen and we  only see the aftermath. 
Example: Alien attacking in real time?…it cuts to someone’s face….then we see the aftermath. 
Happens with the party attack, and soldier attack. 

2. Whats the tone?????????
Its eerie at points, then. Cheerful and cutsey??? Its not even the kids, its the dialogue and music.
Example: The brother gets abducted in ep 2s ending,and the music goes techno? As if ripleys about to pop out and save the day? Are we not supposed to feel dread for the brother???

3. The intro scene crew, THAT seemed so interesting to follow. I have no issue with wendy, or most of the cast/characters. But the introduction scene crew felt established, and had an eerie tone to it especially with the weird android that stalked the women on the ship???? What was that? Creepy. It was a consistent tone.

But all the earth scenes feel kinda upbeat and marvel ish? The plot armor i WAY TO STRONG, the brother totally got stabbed in the leg by the  xeno?  And then he walks like nothing??
Is this a cartooon? 

Alien 1-thriller tone

Alien 2 - action thriller

Alien earth??????
Exposition, eerie yet cutesy tone at times?

Pick one.

The show is not holding back with the graphics, but its still limiting visually with the story telling….how did they manage to do that?
They have punches yet hold back? 
The remains of the attacks are shown, but not the actual attacks???? 
Its confusing


Is the show gonna hold back or not?

Its -pg 13 with action,

And -R  with aftermath of attacks where you see leftovers

replies.author: @douwe4254
replies.text:
Lorewise, they are technical hunters. Meaning, they assess situations and can even detect a trap if its a little too obvious. But they become more stupid in larger groups, as seen in the second movie. Besides that, different ranks, breeds (depending on the original host species). And intelligence depending on its initial survival setting. And they detect the difference between life, and inanimate. Can sense power/electricity (to some degree), are sensitive to heat. Suggesting they also, sorta, feel what we feel, from a hunters perspective. That big brain of theirs does more than we can comprehend. 

If you pay attention even to the movies, more so the non-cannon AvP lore (movies and games) they tend to adapt to the danger they face. Meaning, they are way more aggressive and dangerous towards dangerous targets, and "play" more passively with their food if not. It tends to "show" itself towards people who are terrified, almost in shock. Rarely are they hyper aggressive towards "weaker" prey. Like the brother you mentioned. If you any of the alien games, you see bodies EVERYWHERE. Butchered, for no reason. And once the "herd" is thinned out, they start collecting. Always watching, always observing (unless in groups). Always plotting.

A little like how we play games like assassins creed on higher difficulties, where you are forced to assess the situation, before striking fast and tactically.

In the show's story, this alien just wiped out the entire crew, is hyper aggressive towards humans and likely learned from people trying to fight back on the ship, that it now hunts hyper aggressively simply because it is in a hyper dangerous situation. And will start to stalk, once things settle down. The show will VERY likely include a hive/nest on earth. Where the alien will have to learn to figure out that it does have to kill synthetics or perish itself. We also have a handful of eggs that are not hatched yet. Visually speaking, the show will likely expand into a Alien into Aliens curve, where people will start to control the xenomorphs, the future lore of the series will need to explain why WU still wants to hunt these creatures down for research, assuming it still needs to be trafficked towards earth.

replies.author: @douwe4254
replies.text:
@onastick2411 Rewatch the movies one day, pay attention to how it kills each target. Replay Aliens vs Predator, Alien Isolation, arguable Aliens Dark Descent.

Play out some of the scenes in the games when you walk into a room of bodies. Pay attention to how fast/slow it moves with each target. Shower scenes, always slow, generally always shows itself stabbing the front of the face. 

So to directly answer your question, the alien will hunt appropriately to the plot. If they do it right. Slow and in your face with weaker (scared, hurt) targets. Fast and deadly when surrounded, or facing danger itself. It adapts, faster than people. Its (apex) 'edge'. Faster, smarter, stronger, with every limb a deadly weapon. 

The alien universe, is about the apex predator. Which tied in great with the AvP predator lore. In all the movies, the alien was the apex, with Ripley beating the apex, proving she (humans) could overcome this hyper aggressive and deadly apex. How she could overcome the ultimate danger.

And in the show, we still have the rainbow brigade attempting to tone down their influence, yet we have a female lead - like Ripley - who will eventually become the new Apex. Dare I say predict that we will get scenes where the Alien will eventually run from her, and survive. Scared, of her and the rest of the lost boys.

And dare I say predict, lead into the storyline of the alien building a nest to overcome the danger.

Its a show, there is going to be filler. Even the second movie had moments that were classic for their time. Even in predator you need to sometimes close one eye, and accept that characters do stupid shit.

Including the brother and their team. They are basically the neighborhood watch, first responders. Completely ill equipped for a situation like this. And show, various times to be overly zealous, ignorant to danger.

Imagine their life, on earth. A society controlled by companies, policies and laws. Like overconfident grunts just fresh out of bootcamp, straight into a spaceship. Completely oblivious to animals, the dangers of animals, or the idea that people would bring hyper dangerous species back to earth.

If the writers 'get it', the planet will be forced to adapt, and we see a narrative arc that will grow from naivety towards taking the threat seriously. - Overruled by company greed. Beaten by our AI powergirl.

replies.text:
@vforwombat9915 I actually really like this show, so I think we’re on the same side, and I agree it was a great scene. I really like the aesthetic too. Yea- “just a matter of what you’re willing to accept” - suspension of disbelief. I agree, so the speed isn’t a deal breaker for me, but I’m an argument nerd so I’ll say this:

“The whole alien franchise is unrealistic”. - We often see these arguments on YouTube, etc… well anywhere really. But they are “false equivalence fallacies”… It’s like saying “"Because the story is already fictional, any level of unreality is acceptable.” And ignores the concept of “internal logic within the fictional world”. An example of this would be the same defense used for Superman surviving an atomic bomb. (I’m sure people will use this argument on you in the future for something.) Maybe you can say “maybe the alien was originally quite fast and aggressive, but then it evolved to be slower and stealthier in the future because that was a better strategy” instead. idk, something like that I’d accept 👍🏻 I know you’re overall feeling is probably I should just relax about the speed issue, and you’re probably right! It was my only criticism. Additionally, I found the acting amazing and I’m super excited for part 3.

The original Alien crew basically had a spy (Bishop) and the ship was a towing vessel. It's not unlikely that a scientific research ship with both cyborg and a synth, deployed directly by Weyland Yutani specifically for the mission of finding invasive alien species would have a better suited equipment and weapons for the mission. And yes, both the synth and the cyborg could've developed, or retrofitted weapons to stun the species. They did not kill them, nor were their methods perfect (like the foam for trapping them). One of those ships will have an internet connection, gps tracking of its location, numerous computers just in the personal property of the crew any one of which can download an app to give them all the latest charts as well as the current and tide forecast for any area in the world, as well a radar. The other will have electric lighting, books, paper charts, and might have radar. So yes the Nostromo would be more advanced vastly more advanced.

Search and Rescue crews often have to face unknown obstacles and/or threats when performing their job in dangerous environments. As is appropriate when approaching an opposition Corpo craft, the SAR team was armed and armored for the worst case scenario when they might need to protect themselves or any survivors. replies.author: @bilson7523
replies.text:
​@Joseph-um6wz Hard disagree. I think the universe has a lot of potential if you're willing to step outside the box.

The problem isn't the Xeno, it's that every iteration of Aliens for the last 30 years has been obsessed with recreating Ripley. 

If you go read some writers who have been comfortable with moving away from the Ripley trope, there are a lot of really good stories in this universe: William Gibson's Alien 3 script and The Draconis Strain Saga in Alien RPG, particularly The Destroyer of Worlds, both come to mind as good fodder for "normie storytelling." 

What all these share is the Xeno is a piece of the larger universe. For example, The Destroyer of Worlds revolves a lot around rising tensions between the USCM and the UPP, and a lot of that story revolves around trying to stop some suspected defectors, with the Xeno only becoming relevant later as the motivation for the Sin Eaters to consider defecting.

The problem isn't the Xeno isn't interesting, the problem is the storytelling has been super lazy, wanting to ultimately build to slasher-flick payoffs.

replies.author: @axelhopfinger533
replies.text:
@Joseph-um6wz Also the problem is, that Xenomorphs are only really dangerous when they are still an unknown threat and in specific situations, like isolated habitats like space ships or stations. And if people try to mess with them to weaponize them. Or if they somehow get access to larger, defenseless populations for food and breeding.
By themselves and once known and understood, Xenomorphs are rather easily countered. Like hunting them down with drones and androids. Or nuking them from orbit. Sure, they are somewhat clever beasts and quite stealthy. And their acid blood can make quite a mess of things. But they have very clear limitations in adapting to new threats. They're no Tyranids after all.

@Joseph-um6wz Disagree. There were plenty of parts about the Xenomorphs that the viewers 
were dying to know - but never had the answer. While some movies (like Prometheus) presumed
to take up the mantle of delivering the answers - the kind they provided was akin to "cause i said so",
creating only more dissatisfaction on the audience's side. Throw all that clone-of-my-clone rubbish
away as if it never happened, and create a coherent story about the alien homeworld, the crashed
ship on LV-426, maybe even how did they evolve to be what they are, perhaps through war with
another apex predator species...perhaps they had their own planet somewhere and maybe became
what they are through some kind of catastrophic event.  Another route is to take it further back in 
time and presuppose that there were Xenomorphs of high intelligence capable of creating a kind 
of "convert or die" civilization, which can open further creative options. There is a lot of what can be 
done, if the imagination allows. Though in modern hollywood, imagination is extinct.

H.R. Giger drew the Aliens without thinking about their lore, backstory and whatnot, but he was an
extremely talented mofo, so whatever film-daubers may try to come up with to match his creativity -
it's not gonna be simple, nor easy. The fact remains that in most cases they have miserably failed.
Too bad they don't understand that, or their mistakes.

As much as I love the older movies, I think the franchise is better when it expands beyond the exact same scenarios as the first two. There’s always thematic similarities, but I have to give the Alien universe props for branching out while still retaining the classic plot themes. I know there’s two types of fans though. I didn’t love Romulus despite mostly enjoying Prometheus and liking Covenant.

replies.text:
@00spinz35 I formed my opinions before I watched Drinker's video.  The scene overly boosts the xeno's killing power and massively underscores human capabilities.  It kills 4 armed soldiers watching it in less than 2 seconds.  They show its hand pop out of the cocoon while two soldiers are standing back in clear line of sight of it but they dont react.  The xeno should be terrifying against competent opponents.   Yet scenes earlier it just menaces Hermit from above and cant catch him when its 10 feet behind him before elevator doors open AND close.  Could fix elevator one by having xeno either land above and kill those soldiers first giving him headstart or landing low and having to climb up.

I thought the same thing about baseball room, yea he is dazed BUT it still comes off really weird.  Plus, he doesn't mention to his sister or the guy about the 8 foot tall murder machine.  I would think dazed but before that he jumped into action to try and treat a wound so clearly has enough senses back but still doesn't?  When he meets that soldier before wig party he talks about seeing big animals but hardly emphasizes how terrifying it was and how they should be on the lookout.  Just immediately get distracted by the party

Then of course 8 foot murder machine shows up and he says "move" not "OH FUCK THERE IT IS" but even saying move the guy just stands there like a lamb to slaughter, once again massive lack of competence for humans.  I mean who doesn't even back up when that big bitch is coming in for a kiss.  Xeno takes its time to kill the armed soldier but turbo slaughters the partygoers, then goes back to slow mo for Hermit.  I could fix this scene by just having Hermit notice the Alien when its already right behind the soldier and right as he turns to look it gives him the kiss, then you draw out the party slaughter while Hermit finds a room to hide in(baseball room), alien breaks down door and boom stun gun. text: Let's say the ship enters Earth's atmosphere at the same speed as the Apollo 11 Command Module, roughly 25,000 miles per hour.  The looks like it wasn't designed for a ballistic reentry, so it would probably break up pretty quickly.  A fair amount of mass would burn up, but it looks like a big ship so plenty of it would likely make it through.  The reason all the craters on the Moon are circular even though meteoroids would strike at different angles is because it's not like throwing a rock into some sand; all that kinetic energy is released at once in a massive explosion.  The time from hitting the atmosphere to impact would be a matter of seconds, and I can't imagine a single living thing within a kilometer or so of the impact site would survive.  Thank you fellow nerd for reading all of this.  Let me know if I got anything wrong.

author: @bilson7523
text:
I've said it before and I'll say it again. This was a misstep from the get go. The correct choice for this show was to take "The Destroyer of Worlds" campaign from Alien RPG and just make it a show.

It doesn't break the universe by pushing knowledge of the Alien further back. It sits nicely right between Aliens and Alien 3.
It gives a lot of world building by showing the reality of the UPP and USCM cold war from the Aliens-era.
It's setup is a slow burn that gives a lot of room for character building, because the first 2/3rds of the campaign are all light skirmishes as the USCM is trying to figure out what they're fighting and who in the unit is actually on their side. 
The ending of Destroyer of Worlds is fantastic from a story perspective, lots of tension as the party is trying to escape the planet before the nuclear ordinance setup to destroy evidence wipes them out too. 
It even has a lot of potential for social commentary and a sequel series, as the destruction of Ft. Nebraska ends up being a pivotal justification for multiple stories that followed "Destoryer of Worlds." 

The problem with Alien Earth will always be the implications on the IP. We're now at a point where the two best films in the franchise (Alien and Aliens) are compromised from a setup standpoint.

HOW is the Xeno a relatively unknown creature 70+ years after Alien: Earth?

author: @disgrntledcanadian

Here's the thing with the very concept.

It takes away from the franchise itself.

The underlying fear that drove the franchise is that no matter what, these aliens can NOT reach earth as it would be devastating.

Cut to this prequel as it were that states that this has canonically already happened, thus negating the dread, suspense and horror of everything that follows.

Basically, changing it all to "If these things get to earth, we've handled it before with a bunch of kids so it's no big deal".

replies.author: @JohnSmith-zl1tr
replies.text:
I've only watched the first two and I never really felt that fear I was afraid for the people in the movie but not the people on earth. Plus as Aliens showed the aliens would be mincemeat on earth. They're dangerous to unarmed people and dangerous to unprepared people. But even a very poorly prepared and entirely overconfident force was able to achieve a kill death ratio of something like 10 to 1 when outnumbered 10 to 1. So aliens on future earth would kill a bunch of people in whatever city they landed in and then get shot dead by the local police, and if they lasted longer then that somehow then a small unit of the army/colonial marines would show up and blow them to pieces with ease. Plus once you know how the face huggers work you just shove the victims in a sealed room while you try to help them and any dangers is contained to those already "hugged". So you're looking at a bad month in Chicago worth of murders, which is nothing on a global scale.

Basically, aliens on earth is a like a slow zombie apocalypse in the USA. In theory it's terrifying but in reality there are way too many guns in the country for it to be much worse then a single mass shooter.

The chase scene is a joke with the elevator and could be easily have been written better(like the alien running into a unlucky resident giving him time to get the the elevator while someone takes the hit). BUT the real kicker is what happens after he sees the armed marine get mangled at close range. AND straight after he gets extremely lucky when the creature goes after the cosplaying party idiots, what does he do? No hesitation or any sense of self preservation. Just pick up a rifle and go investigate the apartment for the dragon(no tactical retreat and calling for support). And after waking up, not knowing if the creature has woken up as well and still roaming the halls, what does he do? Go through the rooms of the EXACT same scene of the slaughter like nothing happened. And then take a stroll the memory lane for some bullshit baseball memory with his father. THEN when seeing first person after the horrible ordeal, he starts talking about the baseball shit and never share the danger he just witnessed even after one of the "lost boys" refers it as "a bear". Sure, I guess he is supposed to be a stoic soldier by training, but when seeing a fucking alien rampage a party of people and a fellow soldier, he seems more like a brain dead zombie on heroin. And we the viewers are supposed to go along with this behavior narrative  like it is totally normal or relatable. Give me a fucking break Disney! I also have a bad feeling that the showrunners are gonna steer this ship of a show to the bottom of the ocean in no time if the same logic resumes with the remaining episodes.

replies.author: @thrrax
replies.text: Alien Isolation was inaccurate with its depiction of regular weapons. It wasn't possible to kill the xeno via regular means because it would have defeated the entire point of the game. Not to mention that the AI was cheating by having a xeno tethered to the player at all times, and it would periodically roll dice according to its distance in order to decide if it came after them or not. That's why it was always able to find players even if they hid a few minutes before and remained in their hiding place. The game willingly forced players to always be moving in order to have better chances of putting distance between them and the xeno, so that the dice rolls would be smaller. The game has been datamined since and people discovered all these shenanigans.
